format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
    toc: true

    embed-resources: true

execute:

  message: false
---

# INTRODUCTION

## The Rebalancing Challenge in Philadelphia

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Philadelphia's Indego bike share system faces the same operational challenge as every bike share system: **rebalancing bikes to meet anticipated demand**.
:::

## Structure of Assignment

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
**Part 1 :** Indego Trip Data (Q1 2025)
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(tidycensus)
library(riem)  # For Philadelphia weather from ASOS stations

library(viridis) # color scales for visualization
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(patchwork)


library(here)
options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier

```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
plotTheme <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks    = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

mapTheme <- theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position  = "right",
    plot.margin      = margin(1, 1, 1, 1, "cm"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1, "cm"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

palette5 <- c("#eff3ff", "#bdd7e7", "#6baed6", "#3182bd", "#08519c")
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

census_api_key(Sys.getenv("CENSUS_API_KEY"))

#Use the following code if you're not able to access the key in your environment
#census_api_key("yourkeyhere", install=TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
```

# IMPORTING, PREPARING & Loading Data

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
For this assignment, the first step involves importing the dataset titled “Loading Indego Trip Data (Q1 2025)” into the working environment. This dataset contains detailed records of bike-share trips for the first quarter (January to March) of 2025. To begin the data preparation process, the file is loaded using appropriate functions (such as read.csv() or readr::read_csv() in R), ensuring that all variables—such as trip duration, start and end times, station locations, and user types—are correctly read into the system. Proper importing is essential because it allows us to inspect the structure, verify data types, and identify any inconsistencies or missing values early on. Once successfully loaded, the dataset becomes ready for further cleaning, transformation, and analysis in subsequent steps.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Read Q1 2025 data
indego <- read_csv(here("D:/Ph.D/US Pakistan Knowledge Corridore/Upenn/Subjects/Public Policy Analytics/week 11/indego-trips-2025-q1.csv"))

# Quick look at the data

```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Look at the data. What do you suspect the coordinate system is? What do you notice about the date and time structure?
:::

## Examine the Data Structure

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
From observing the dataset, the coordinate system appears to be geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude), most likely based on the WGS 84 standard. This is suggested by the values in the start_lat, start_lon, end_lat, and end_lon columns, which fall within the typical range for latitude (around 39°) and longitude (around -75°), consistent with locations in the Philadelphia area.
:::

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Regarding the date and time structure, both start_time and end_time are stored in a combined date-time format (e.g., 1/1/2025 0:05), where the date and time are not separated into different fields. The time is represented in a 24-hour format, and the structure does not include seconds, only hours and minutes. Additionally, the format appears to follow a month/day/year convention, which is common in the United States. This combined format will likely require parsing or conversion into a proper datetime object for easier analysis, such as extracting hours, days, or calculating trip durations more precisely.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# How many trips?
cat("Total trips in Q1 2025:", nrow(indego), "\n")

# Date range
cat("Date range:", 
    min(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "to", 
    max(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "\n")

# How many unique stations?
cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego$start_station)), "\n")

# Trip types
table(indego$trip_route_category)

# Passholder types
table(indego$passholder_type)

# Bike types
table(indego$bike_type)
```

## Create Time Bins

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We need to aggregate trips into hourly intervals for our panel data structure. Read up on the lubridate library for more information on the date/time data extraction.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
indego <- indego %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse datetime
    start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time),
    end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time),
    
    # Create hourly bins
    interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"),
    
    # Extract time features
    week = week(interval60),
    month = month(interval60, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval60),
    date = as.Date(interval60),
    
    # Create useful indicators
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

# Look at temporal features
head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend))

```

**Question** Explain the interval60 feature to someone else at your table.

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
interval60 is an hourly time grouping variable. It takes the exact trip start time and rounds it down to the beginning of that hour, so that all trips occurring within the same hour are grouped together for analysis. Example: If a trip starts at 8:45 AM, interval60 will store it as 8:00 AM, meaning it belongs to the 8–9 AM time bin.
:::

#EDA OF INDEGO DATA

## Trips Over Time

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Daily trip counts
daily_trips <- indego %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n())

ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q1 2025",
    subtitle = "Winter demand patterns in Philadelphia",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Daily Trips",
    caption = "Source: Indego bike share"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
**Question:** What patterns do you see? How does ridership change over time?
:::
::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Ridership shows a clear upward trend from January to March/early April, as indicated by the rising dashed line, while also displaying a strong cyclical pattern with regular peaks and dips that likely reflect higher weekday commuting and lower weekend usage; at the same time, occasional sharp spikes and drops suggest the influence of external factors such as weather or special events, meaning overall demand is steadily increasing but remains highly variable over time.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
fly_eagles_fly <- daily_trips %>% filter(date == "2025-02-14")

typical_boring_friday <- indego %>%
  filter(dotw == "Fri", date != "2025-02-14") %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n()) %>%
  summarize(avg_friday_trips = mean(trips))

print(fly_eagles_fly)
print(typical_boring_friday)

```

## Hourly Patterns

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Average trips by hour and day type
hourly_patterns <- indego %>%
  group_by(hour, weekend) %>%
  summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))

ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns",
    subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Average Trips per Hour",
    color = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
**Question:** When are the peak hours? How do weekends differ from weekdays?
:::
::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
-Peak hours on weekdays occur during the morning (around 8–9 AM) and evening (around 5–6 PM), reflecting typical commuting patterns. In contrast, weekends show a single, broader peak in the afternoon (around 1–4 PM), with lower activity in the morning. This indicates that weekday ridership is driven by work-related travel, while weekend usage is more leisure-oriented and spread out over the day.
:::

## Top Stations

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'asis'
# Most popular origin stations
top_stations <- indego %>%
  count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>%
  arrange(desc(trips)) %>%
  head(20)

kable(top_stations, 
      caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px")
```

# PUTTING DATA IN CONTEXT

## Loading Philadelphia Census Data

We are getting census tract data to add demographic context to our stations.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Get Philadelphia census tracts
philly_census <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    "B01003_001",  # Total population
    "B19013_001",  # Median household income
    "B08301_001",  # Total commuters
    "B08301_010",  # Commute by transit
    "B02001_002",  # White alone
    "B25077_001"   # Median home value
  ),
  state = "PA",
  county = "Philadelphia",
  year = 2022,
  geometry = TRUE,
  output = "wide"
) %>%
  rename(
    Total_Pop = B01003_001E,
    Med_Inc = B19013_001E,
    Total_Commuters = B08301_001E,
    Transit_Commuters = B08301_010E,
    White_Pop = B02001_002E,
    Med_Home_Value = B25077_001E
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Taking_Transit = (Transit_Commuters / Total_Commuters) * 100,
    Percent_White = (White_Pop / Total_Pop) * 100
  ) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)  #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83



```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'asis'


kable(head(philly_census, 20), 
      caption = "Philadelphia Census Data by tracts",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
                full_width = TRUE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px", width = "100%")

```

## Map Philadelphia Context

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Map median income
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  # Stations 
  geom_point(
    data = indego,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  mapTheme
```

**Question** These data are in WGS84. If you wanted to calculate a spatial distance-based variable, like a buffer, should you use the dataset as it?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
No, you should not use the dataset in WGS84 to calculate spatial distance-based variables like buffers because WGS84 is a geographic coordinate system that measures locations in degrees rather than consistent distance units; this can lead to inaccurate distance calculations. Instead, you should first transform the data into a projected coordinate system (such as a local UTM or state plane projection) that uses linear units like meters or feet, which ensures that distances and buffers are calculated correctly.
:::

## Join Census Data to Stations

We'll spatially join census characteristics to each bike station.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Create sf object for stations
stations_sf <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join to get census tract for each station
stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

# Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts.

stations_for_map <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Add back to trip data
indego_census <- indego %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )


# Prepare data for visualization
stations_for_map <- indego %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Create the map showing problem stations
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar,
    na.value = "grey90"
  ) +
  # Stations with census data (small grey dots)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)",
    caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts"
  ) +
  mapTheme



```

## Dealing with missing data

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We need to decide what to do with the non-residential bike share stations. For this example, we are going to remove them -- this is not necessarily the right way to do things always, but for the sake of simplicity, we are narrowing our scope to only stations in residential neighborhoods. We might opt to create a separate model for non-residential stations..
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Identify which stations to keep
valid_stations <- stations_census %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Filter trip data to valid stations only
indego_census <- indego %>%
  filter(start_station %in% valid_stations) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )
```

## Getting Weather Data

Weather significantly affects bike share demand! Let's get hourly weather for Philadelphia.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL)
# This covers Q1 2025: January 1 - March 31
weather_data <- riem_measures(
  station = "PHL",  # Philadelphia International Airport
  date_start = "2025-01-01",
  date_end = "2025-03-31"
)

# Process weather data
weather_processed <- weather_data %>%
  mutate(
    interval60 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"),
    Temperature = tmpf,  # Temperature in Fahrenheit
    Precipitation = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i),  # Hourly precip in inches
    Wind_Speed = sknt  # Wind speed in knots
  ) %>%
  select(interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed) %>%
  distinct(interval60, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed
weather_complete <- weather_processed %>%
  complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>%
  fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down")

# Look at the weather
summary(weather_complete %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed))
```

## Visualize Weather Patterns

Who is glad it's Spring!?

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
ggplot(weather_complete, aes(x = interval60, y = Temperature)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q1 2025",
    subtitle = "Winter to early spring transition",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The plot shows that temperatures start relatively cool in January, decline into a colder period in late January to early February, and then gradually increase through March into early spring, as reflected by the upward-sloping red trend line. While there is substantial short-term fluctuation day to day, the overall pattern is a clear seasonal transition from winter to warmer spring conditions, with temperatures becoming both higher and slightly more variable toward the end of the period.
:::

# CREATE SPACE-TIME PANEL

## Aggregate Trips to Station-Hour Level

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Count trips by station-hour
trips_panel <- indego_census %>%
  group_by(interval60, start_station, start_lat, start_lon,
           Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop) %>%
  summarize(Trip_Count = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

# How many station-hour observations?
nrow(trips_panel)
# How many unique stations?
length(unique(trips_panel$start_station))

# How many unique hours?
length(unique(trips_panel$interval60))

hist(trips_panel$Trip_Count)
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
This histogram shows that Trip_Count is highly right-skewed. Most observations are concentrated at very low values (around 1–3 trips), which explains the tall bars on the left side. As the number of trips increases, the frequency drops sharply, creating a long tail extending to higher values (up to about 25). This indicates that while most individuals make only a few trips, a small number make significantly more, suggesting the presence of outliers or heavy users.
:::

## Create Complete Panel Structure
::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Not every station has trips every hour. We need a **complete panel** where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0).
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Calculate expected panel size
n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel$start_station))
n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel$interval60))
expected_rows <- n_stations * n_hours

cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows, big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows - nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n")

# Create complete panel
study_panel <- expand_grid(
  interval60 = unique(trips_panel$interval60),
  start_station = unique(trips_panel$start_station)
) %>%
  # Join trip counts
  left_join(trips_panel %>% 
              select(interval60, start_station, Trip_Count),
            by = c("interval60", "start_station")) %>%
  # Replace NA trip counts with 0
  mutate(Trip_Count = replace_na(Trip_Count, 0))

hist(study_panel$Trip_Count)

# Fill in station attributes (they're the same for all hours)
station_attributes <- trips_panel %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  summarize(
    start_lat = first(start_lat),
    start_lon = first(start_lon),
    Med_Inc = first(Med_Inc),
    Percent_Taking_Transit = first(Percent_Taking_Transit),
    Percent_White = first(Percent_White),
    Total_Pop = first(Total_Pop)
  )



study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  left_join(station_attributes, by = "start_station")


# Verify we have complete panel
cat("Complete panel rows:", format(nrow(study_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
This histogram also shows a strong right-skewed distribution of Trip_Count. The majority of observations are clustered at very low values (mostly 0–2 trips), with frequencies dropping rapidly as the number of trips increases. A long tail extends toward higher values, indicating that only a small proportion of individuals make many trips. This suggests the presence of a few high-trip outliers while most individuals have low trip counts.
:::

## Adding Time Features

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We are enhancing the dataset by adding time-based features extracted from the interval60 variable. These include week, month, day of the week, hour, and date, which help capture temporal patterns in the data. Additionally, we create indicator variables for weekend (Saturday and Sunday) and rush hours (peak travel times in the morning and evening). These features will improve analysis and modeling by incorporating time-related behavioral trends in trip activity.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  mutate(
    week = week(interval60),
    month = month(interval60, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval60),
    date = as.Date(interval60),
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )


```

## Joining Weather Data

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We are integrating weather data into the main dataset by joining study_panel with weather_complete using the common time variable interval60. This allows us to enrich trip data with environmental factors such as temperature and precipitation, which may influence travel behavior. After merging, we inspect the dataset structure and check for any missing values in key variables to ensure data quality for further analysis.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  left_join(weather_complete, by = "interval60")


# Check for missing values
summary(study_panel %>% select(Trip_Count, Temperature, Precipitation))
```

# CREATE TEMPORAL LAG VARIABLES

The key innovation for space-time prediction is that past demand predicts future demand.

## Why Temporal Lags?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be \~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions.We still can't use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic. We are enhancing the dataset by creating temporal lag features, which capture how past trip demand influences future demand. After sorting the data by station and time, we generate lag variables (e.g., 1 hour, 2 hours, 3 hours, 12 hours, and 1 day) within each station to reflect temporal patterns in trip activity. These features help improve predictive modeling by leveraging the idea that past behavior is a strong indicator of future demand. Finally, rows with missing lag values are removed to ensure a complete dataset for analysis.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Sort by station and time
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval60)

# Create lag variables WITHIN each station
study_panel <- study_panel %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1),
    lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2),
    lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3),
    lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12),
    lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station)
study_panel_complete <- study_panel %>%
  filter(!is.na(lag1day))

cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```

## Visualize Lag Correlations

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Sample one station to visualize
example_station <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>%
  head(168)  # One week

# Plot actual vs lagged demand
ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Current" = "#08519c",
    "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd",
    "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station",
    subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand",
    x = "Date-Time",
    y = "Trip Count",
    color = "Time Period"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
This chart shows how trip demand at a single station changes over time and compares current demand with demand from one hour earlier and 24 hours earlier. The goal is to see whether past activity helps predict present usage. The small, scattered spikes and frequent zeros indicate that demand at this station is generally low and irregular. While there are occasional similarities—especially between current demand and the same time the previous day—these patterns are not very strong, suggesting only limited predictability. Overall, past demand provides some insight, but the station’s usage is fairly inconsistent.
:::

# TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT

We will train on PAST data and test on FUTURE data!

## Why Temporal Validation Matters

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
In real operations, at 6:00 AM on March 15, we need to predict demand for March 15-31. We have data from Jan 1 - March 14, but NOT from March 15-31 (it hasn't happened yet!).
:::
**Wrong approach:** Train on weeks 10-13, test on weeks 1-9 (predicting past from future!)
**Correct approach:** Train on weeks 1-9, test on weeks 10-13 (predicting future from past)

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Split by week
# Q1 has weeks 1-13 (Jan-Mar)
# Train on weeks 1-9 (Jan 1 - early March)
# Test on weeks 10-13 (rest of March)

# Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods?
early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(week < 10) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)


late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(week >= 10) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods
common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations)


# Filter panel to only common stations
study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(start_station %in% common_stations)

# NOW create train/test split
train <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(week < 10)

test <- study_panel_complete %>%
  filter(week >= 10)

cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Training date range:", min(train$date), "to", max(train$date), "\n")
cat("Testing date range:", min(test$date), "to", max(test$date), "\n")

```

-Training observations: 352,240 
-Testing observations: 153,748 
-Training date range: 20090 to 20151 
-Testing date range: 20152 to 20178



# BUILDING PREDICTIVE MODELS

We'll build 5 models with increasing complexity to see what improves predictions.

## Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather)

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
train <- train %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(train$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

# Now run the model
model1 <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation,
  data = train
)


```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The model uses Monday as the baseline. Each coefficient represents the difference in expected trips per station-hour compared to Monday (dotw_simple2 = Tuesday, etc.).
:::

**Weekday Pattern (Tue-Fri):**
-All weekdays have positive coefficients (0.033 to 0.059) -Wednesday has the highest weekday effect (+0.059) -Weekdays likely benefit from consistent commuting and work-related travel patterns
**Weekend Pattern (Sat-Sun):**
-Both weekend days have negative coefficients (-0.065 and -0.063) -This means fewer trips per station-hour than Monday, reflecting reduced commuting demand  
**Hourly Interpretation**

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The model uses hour 0 (midnight) as the baseline. Early morning hours (1–4) have negative coefficients (around -0.025 to -0.053), indicating lower activity than midnight. Activity begins to rise at hour 5 (+0.023) and increases sharply during the morning commute, reaching a peak at hour 8 (+0.550). Demand remains relatively high through late morning and afternoon, then rises again to the highest peak at hour 17 (+0.615), representing the evening rush. After this, trip activity steadily declines into the night, with very low levels by hour 23 (+0.042).
:::



## Model 2: Add Temporal Lags

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
model2 <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day,
  data = train
)


```

**Question:** Did adding lags improve R²? Why or why not?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Yes, adding temporal lag variables substantially improved the model’s R². In Model 1, the R² is 0.0788, meaning the model explains only about 7.9% of the variation in trip counts. After including lag variables in Model 2, the R² increases to 0.2293, indicating that about 22.9% of the variation is now explained. This improvement occurs because lag variables (such as 1-hour and 1-day lags) capture temporal dependence, meaning past trip demand strongly predicts future demand. Since bike usage follows consistent daily and hourly patterns, incorporating these past values provides much more predictive power than relying only on time, weather, and day-of-week variables.
:::

## Model 3: Add Demographics

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
model3 <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White,
  data = train
)


```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Adding the socioeconomic variables in Model 3 leads to a small improvement in R² compared to Model 2. Specifically, R² increases from 0.2293 in Model 2 to 0.2339 in Model 3, indicating only a modest gain in explanatory power. This suggests that while variables like median income, transit usage, and racial composition do have statistically significant effects, they add relatively little new information beyond what is already captured by temporal patterns and lag variables. The reason is that trip demand is primarily driven by strong temporal dynamics (captured by lag variables), whereas socioeconomic characteristics are more static and explain less variation at the hourly level. Thus, Model 3 improves performance slightly, but not nearly as dramatically as when temporal lags were introduced in Model 2.
:::

## Model 4: Add Station Fixed Effects

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
model4 <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White +
    as.factor(start_station),
  data = train
)

# Summary too long with all station dummies, just show key metrics
#cat("Model 4 R-squared:", summary(model4)$r.squared, "\n")
#cat("Model 4 Adj R-squared:", summary(model4)$adj.r.squared, "\n")
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Adding the new variables in Model 4 results in a noticeable improvement in R² compared to Model 3. The R² increases from 0.2339 in Model 3 to 0.2623 in Model 4, indicating a meaningful gain in explanatory power. This suggests that the additional features included in Model 4 capture important variation in trip demand that was not explained by temporal, lag, and socioeconomic variables alone. Unlike the small improvement seen when moving from Model 2 to Model 3, this larger increase implies that Model 4 introduces variables with stronger predictive relevance. Overall, Model 4 provides a better fit, showing that these newly added factors contribute significantly to explaining bike trip patterns.
:::

## Model 5: Add Rush Hour Interaction

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
model5 <- lm(
  Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + rush_hour + as.factor(month) +
    Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White +
    as.factor(start_station) +
    rush_hour * weekend,  # Rush hour effects different on weekends
  data = train
)

#cat("Model 5 R-squared:", summary(model5)$r.squared, "\n")
#cat("Model 5 Adj R-squared:", summary(model5)$adj.r.squared, "\n")
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Adding the new variables in Model 5 results in only a slight improvement in R² compared to Model 4. Specifically, R² increases from 0.2623 in Model 4 to 0.2672 in Model 5, indicating a modest gain in explanatory power. This suggests that the additional features in Model 5 contribute some new information, but their overall impact is relatively limited. Compared to the larger improvement observed from Model 3 to Model 4, this smaller increase implies diminishing returns, where most of the key variation in trip demand has already been captured by earlier models. Overall, Model 5 provides a slightly better fit, but the improvement is incremental rather than substantial.
:::

# MODEL EVALUATION

## Calculate Predictions and MAE
```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Get predictions on test set

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
test <- test %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(test$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

test <- test %>%
  mutate(
    pred1 = predict(model1, newdata = test),
    pred2 = predict(model2, newdata = test),
    pred3 = predict(model3, newdata = test),
    pred4 = predict(model4, newdata = test),
    pred5 = predict(model5, newdata = test)
  )

# Calculate MAE for each model
mae_results <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction"
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred1), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred2), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred3), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred4), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred5), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'asis'

kable(mae_results, 
      digits = 2,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The comparison of Mean Absolute Error (MAE) across models shows a clear improvement when temporal lag variables are introduced, followed by only minor gains from additional features. Model 1 (Time + Weather) has the highest error at 0.62, indicating relatively weaker predictive performance. When temporal lags are added in Model 2, MAE drops significantly to 0.54, demonstrating that past demand greatly improves prediction accuracy. Further additions, including demographics (Model 3), station fixed effects (Model 4), and the Rush Hour interaction (Model 5), lead to only slight improvements, reducing MAE marginally to 0.53. This pattern suggests that while these additional variables do contribute some predictive value, most of the accuracy gains come from capturing temporal dynamics, and later models show diminishing returns in improving prediction error.The comparison of Mean Absolute Error (MAE) across models shows a clear improvement when temporal lag variables are introduced, followed by only minor gains from additional features. Model 1 (Time + Weather) has the highest error at 0.62, indicating relatively weaker predictive performance. When temporal lags are added in Model 2, MAE drops significantly to 0.54, demonstrating that past demand greatly improves prediction accuracy. Further additions, including demographics (Model 3), station fixed effects (Model 4), and the Rush Hour interaction (Model 5), lead to only slight improvements, reducing MAE marginally to 0.53. This pattern suggests that while these additional variables do contribute some predictive value, most of the accuracy gains come from capturing temporal dynamics, and later models show diminishing returns in improving prediction error.
:::

## Visualize Model Comparison

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
ggplot(mae_results, aes(x = reorder(Model, -MAE), y = MAE)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(MAE, 2)), vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Model Performance Comparison",
    subtitle = "Lower MAE = Better Predictions",
    x = "Model",
    y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)"
  ) +
  plotTheme +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

**Question:** Which features gave us the biggest improvement?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The features that gave the biggest improvement were the temporal lag variables. This is evident from the sharp drop in MAE from 0.62 in Model 1 to 0.54 in Model 2, which is a much larger improvement compared to the minimal reductions seen in later models (only down to 0.53). This occurs because lag variables directly capture temporal dependence—past trip demand is a strong predictor of future demand. In contrast, additional features like demographics, station fixed effects, and Rush Hour contribute only small incremental improvements, indicating that most of the predictive power comes from the temporal patterns in the data.
:::

# SPACE-TIME ERROR ANALYSIS

## Observed vs. Predicted  
Let's use our best model (Model 2) for error analysis.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

test <- test %>%
  mutate(
    error = Trip_Count - pred2,
    abs_error = abs(error),
    time_of_day = case_when(
      hour < 7 ~ "Overnight",
      hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush",
      hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day",
      hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush",
      hour > 18 ~ "Evening"
    )
  )

# Scatter plot by time and day type
ggplot(test, aes(x = Trip_Count, y = pred2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") +
  facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs. Predicted Bike Trips",
    subtitle = "Model 2 performance by time period",
    x = "Observed Trips",
    y = "Predicted Trips",
    caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

**Question:** Where is the model performing well? Where is it struggling?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Using Model 2, the model performs best during mid-day and overnight periods, where most points are closer to the red 45-degree line (perfect prediction) and the spread is relatively tight. This indicates that for low to moderate trip counts, the model predicts reasonably well with smaller errors. However, the model struggles during AM and PM rush hours, as well as the evening period. In these time slots, there is a wider spread of points and many observations fall below the red line, meaning the model tends to underpredict high trip counts. The gap between the green fitted line and the red perfect prediction line is also larger in these periods, showing that the model has difficulty capturing peak demand. Overall, the model handles low-demand periods better but has trouble accurately predicting high-demand spikes during busy hours.
:::

## Spatial Error Patterns

Are prediction errors clustered in certain parts of Philadelphia?

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Calculate MAE by station
station_errors <- test %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_demand = mean(Trip_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

## Create Two Maps Side-by-Side with Proper Legends (sorry these maps are ugly)

# Calculate station errors
station_errors <- test %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred2)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs(Trip_Count - pred2), na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_demand = mean(Trip_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

# Map 1: Prediction Errors
p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = MAE),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "plasma",
    name = "MAE\n(trips)",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5),  # Fewer, cleaner breaks
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Prediction Errors",
       subtitle = "Higher in Center City") +
  mapTheme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 1.5,
    barheight = 12,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))

# Map 2: Average Demand
p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = avg_demand),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Avg\nDemand",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5),  # Clear breaks
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5", "2.0", "2.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Average Demand",
       subtitle = "Trips per station-hour") +
  mapTheme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 1.5,
    barheight = 12,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))




# Map 1: Prediction Errors
p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = MAE),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "plasma",
    name = "MAE (trips)",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5),
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Prediction Errors") +
  mapTheme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 12,
    barheight = 1,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))

# Map 2: Average Demand  
p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = avg_demand),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Avg Demand (trips/hour)",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5),
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5", "2.0", "2.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Average Demand") +
  mapTheme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 12,
    barheight = 1,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))

# Combine
(p1 | p2)

```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Yes, the map shows a clear pattern of spatial clustering in prediction errors. The errors are not randomly distributed; instead, they are concentrated in specific areas. The highest errors appear in the central urban neighborhoods, where the points are colored in darker shades (orange to purple), indicating larger MAE values. These areas likely correspond to dense, high-activity zones where demand is more volatile and harder to predict. In contrast, the outer or peripheral neighborhoods show lighter colors (yellow), suggesting lower errors and better model performance. Overall, the model performs well in less busy areas but struggles in central locations with higher and more variable demand.
:::

## Temporal Error Patterns

When are we most wrong? 

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
he model’s errors vary clearly by time of day, showing distinct temporal patterns. It is most wrong during peak commuting periods, especially the PM rush, which has the highest MAE, followed by the AM rush. This indicates the model struggles to accurately capture sharp increases in demand during busy hours. Errors are moderate during mid-day and evening, where demand is more stable but still somewhat variable. In contrast, the model performs best overnight, with the lowest errors, reflecting more consistent and low demand levels. Overall, prediction accuracy decreases as demand becomes more volatile, particularly during rush hours.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# MAE by time of day and day type
temporal_errors <- test %>%
  group_by(time_of_day, weekend) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))

ggplot(temporal_errors, aes(x = time_of_day, y = MAE, fill = day_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) +
  labs(
    title = "Prediction Errors by Time Period",
    subtitle = "When is the model struggling most?",
    x = "Time of Day",
    y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)",
    fill = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

```

## Errors and Demographics

Are prediction errors related to neighborhood characteristics?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Yes, prediction errors show some relationship with neighborhood demographic characteristics, but the effects are relatively modest. Errors tend to increase slightly with median income, indicating that the model struggles a bit more in higher-income areas. Similarly, there is a positive relationship with the percentage of White residents, suggesting somewhat higher errors in those neighborhoods. In contrast, errors decrease as transit usage increases, meaning the model performs better in areas where more people rely on public transit. Overall, these relationships exist but are not very strong, indicating that demographics explain only a small portion of the variation in prediction errors compared to temporal factors.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Join demographic data to station errors
station_errors_demo <- station_errors %>%
  left_join(
    station_attributes %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc))

# Create plots
p1 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Med_Inc, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Median Income", x = "Median Income", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

p2 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_Taking_Transit, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Transit Usage", x = "% Taking Transit", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

p3 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_White, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Race", x = "% White", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | plot_spacer())
```


<div style="text-align: justify;">**Critical Question:** Are prediction errors systematically higher in certain demographic groups? What are the equity implications? </div>


::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The plots suggest that prediction errors are somewhat systematically related to demographic characteristics, but the patterns are relatively weak rather than extreme. Errors tend to be slightly higher in higher-income neighborhoods and in areas with a higher percentage of White residents, while they are lower in neighborhoods with higher transit usage. This indicates that the model performs a bit better in transit-reliant areas and slightly worse in more affluent or less transit-dependent neighborhoods.

From an equity perspective, this pattern does not strongly indicate that disadvantaged groups are being disproportionately affected by higher errors. In fact, if anything, errors are somewhat higher in more advantaged areas. However, the differences are small, suggesting that demographic factors are not a major driver of model bias. The main takeaway is that while there are minor systematic differences, prediction errors are driven much more by temporal demand patterns than by neighborhood demographics, and there is no clear evidence of significant inequitable performance across groups.
:::

# NOW PART-2 OF ASSIGNMENT

<div style="text-align: justify;"> **Part 2 ** - Downloaded a different quarter of Indego data (Q4 2025) - Applied the same workflow as above and wrote comprehensive report </div>

# IMPORTED AND PREPEARED DATA FIRST

## Loading Indego Trip Data (Q4 2025)



```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Read Q4 2025 data
indego4 <- read_csv(here("D:/Ph.D/US Pakistan Knowledge Corridore/Upenn/Subjects/Public Policy Analytics/Public-Policy-Analytics/lab_5/indego-trips-2025-q4.csv"))



kable(head(indego4, 200), 
      caption = "Indego-trips-2025-q4",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
                full_width = TRUE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px", width = "100%")


```

<div style="text-align: justify;"> Looking at the coordinate columns start_lat, start_lon, end_lat, and end_lon, the values fall around 39.9° latitude and -75.1° to -75.2° longitude, which is immediately recognizable as the Philadelphia, PA region. This confirms the coordinate system is standard WGS84 (EPSG:4326) — the familiar decimal degrees system used by GPS and most web mapping platforms, with negative longitude indicating the Western Hemisphere.
As for the date and time structure, the start_time and end_time columns appear to store both date and time together in a single field formatted as M/D/YYYY H:MM, for example 10/1/2025 0:00. All visible rows fall on October 1, 2025, suggesting this may be a single day's snapshot or the dataset begins from that date. The time portion is in 24-hour format and captures minute-level precision, which allows for calculating trip duration directly — and indeed the duration column already reflects the difference in minutes between start and end times. One thing worth noting is that since date and time are combined in one column, you would need to parse it using lubridate functions like mdy_hm() in R before doing any time-based analysis such as extracting hour of day, day of week, or week number. </div>

## Examine the Data Structure

<div style="text-align: justify;"> Looking at the dataset, it contains 325,361 total trips recorded in Q4 2025 across 294 unique start stations throughout the Philadelphia Indego bike-share network.
Regarding the start_time and end_time columns, the date range showing values like 1759276800 to 1167225420 confirms that these columns are actually stored as Unix timestamps (seconds since January 1, 1970) rather than character strings as previously suspected. This means conversion should be done using as.POSIXct() with origin = "1970-01-01" rather than lubridate::mdy_hm(), which changes our earlier assessment.
The trip_route_category column breaks down into 308,569 One Way trips and 16,792 Round Trip trips, confirming it is a categorical variable and should be converted to a factor. One Way trips dominate heavily, accounting for roughly 95% of all rides.
The plan_duration column reveals five membership categories — Day Pass (8,988), Indego30 (168,054), Indego365 (127,625), Walk-up (20,677), and NULL (17) — rather than just raw day numbers. Indego30 is the most popular pass type, followed closely by Indego365, together accounting for nearly 90% of all trips. The 17 NULL values represent missing or unclassified membership data that may need handling during cleaning.
The bike_id or a similar column distinguishes between electric (225,474) and standard (99,887) bikes, showing that electric bikes now make up roughly 69% of all trips, reflecting a strong shift toward e-bike usage in the system.</div>


```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# How many trips?s
cat("Total trips in Q4 2025:", nrow(indego4), "\n")

# Date range
cat("Date range:", 
    min(mdy_hm(indego4$start_time)), "to", 
    max(mdy_hm(indego4$start_time)), "\n")

# How many unique stations?
cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego4$start_station)), "\n")

# Trip types
table(indego4$trip_route_category)

# Passholder types
table(indego4$passholder_type)

# Bike types
table(indego4$bike_type)
```

## Create Time Bins

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We need to aggregate trips into hourly intervals for our panel data structure. Read up on the lubridate library for more information on the date/time data extraction.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
indego4 <- indego4 %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse datetime
    start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time),
    end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time),
    
    # Create hourly bins
    interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"),
    
    # Extract time features
    week = week(interval60),
    month = month(interval60, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval60),
    date = as.Date(interval60),
    
    # Create useful indicators
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

# Look at temporal features
head(indego4 %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend))

```

**Question** Explaining the interval60 feature:

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
-interval60 is an hourly time grouping variable. It takes the exact trip start time and rounds it down to the beginning of that hour, so that all trips occurring within the same hour are grouped together for analysis. Example: If a trip starts at 8:45 AM, interval60 will store it as 8:00 AM, meaning it belongs to the 8–9 AM time bin.
:::

# EDA OF INDEGO DATA

## Trips Over Time

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Daily trip counts
daily_trips4 <- indego4 %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n())

ggplot(daily_trips4, aes(x = date, y = trips)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q4 2025",
    subtitle = "Winter demand patterns in Philadelphia",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Daily Trips",
    caption = "Source: Indego bike share"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

**Question:** What patterns do you see? How does ridership change over time?

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Ridership shows a clear downward trend from October to January, likely due to colder weather and seasonal effects. While there is significant day-to-day variation, overall usage steadily declines over time. Sharp drops are visible around late December, likely due to holidays such as Christmas and New Year, which reduce commuting and overall bike usage.

Ridership patterns in Q1 and Q4 show opposite seasonal trends. In Q1 (January to April), ridership exhibits a clear upward trend, increasing over time as weather conditions improve and daylight hours extend. In contrast, Q4 (October to January) shows a steady decline in ridership due to colder temperatures, shorter days, and less favorable cycling conditions.

Both quarters display significant daily fluctuations; however, Q1 includes a notable positive outlier (mid-February spike due to a major city event), while Q4 features sharp declines around late December, likely due to holidays such as Christmas and New Year. Overall, the comparison highlights strong seasonality in bike usage, with higher demand in warmer months and reduced activity in colder periods.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
fly_eagles_fly4 <- daily_trips4 %>% filter(date == "2025-02-14")

typical_boring_friday4 <- indego4 %>%
  filter(dotw == "Fri", date != "2025-02-14") %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n()) %>%
  summarize(avg_friday_trips = mean(trips))

print(fly_eagles_fly4)
print(typical_boring_friday4)

```

## Hourly Patterns

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Average trips by hour and day type
hourly_patterns4 <- indego4 %>%
  group_by(hour, weekend) %>%
  summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))

ggplot(hourly_patterns4, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns",
    subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Average Trips per Hour",
    color = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

<div style="text-align: justify;">**Question:** When are the peak hours? How do weekends differ from weekdays?</div>

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Peak hours on weekdays occur around 8–9 AM and 5–6 PM, with the evening peak being the highest, reflecting strong commuting patterns. In contrast, weekends show a single, broader peak in the afternoon (around 2–4 PM) with lower activity in the morning. This indicates that weekday ridership is driven by work-related travel, while weekend usage is more evenly distributed and leisure-oriented. Ridership patterns in Q1 and Q4 show similar daily behavioral structures but differ significantly in intensity and seasonal context. In both quarters, weekdays exhibit a clear bimodal pattern with peaks in the morning (around 8–9 AM) and evening (around 5–6 PM), reflecting commuting activity, while weekends display a single, broader afternoon peak indicative of leisure use. However, Q1 shows lower overall ridership levels and less pronounced peaks, suggesting reduced demand during colder winter months. In contrast, Q4 demonstrates substantially higher ridership and sharper peaks—especially during weekday evening hours—indicating stronger commuting activity and greater overall usage. This difference highlights the impact of seasonal variation, where more favorable conditions in Q4 lead to increased bike usage, even though the underlying daily patterns remain consistent across both periods.
:::

## Top Stations

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
#| # Most popular origin stations
top_stations4 <- indego4 %>%
  count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>%
  arrange(desc(trips)) %>%
  head(20)

kable(top_stations4, 
      caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

# PUTTING DATA IN CONTEXT

## We are Loading Philadelphia Census Data again

<div style="text-align: justify;"> We'll get census tract data again although it is not necessary but we are doing it again to add demographic context to our stations.</div>

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Get Philadelphia census tracts
philly_census4 <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    "B01003_001",  # Total population
    "B19013_001",  # Median household income
    "B08301_001",  # Total commuters
    "B08301_010",  # Commute by transit
    "B02001_002",  # White alone
    "B25077_001"   # Median home value
  ),
  state = "PA",
  county = "Philadelphia",
  year = 2022,
  geometry = TRUE,
  output = "wide"
) %>%
  rename(
    Total_Pop = B01003_001E,
    Med_Inc = B19013_001E,
    Total_Commuters = B08301_001E,
    Transit_Commuters = B08301_010E,
    White_Pop = B02001_002E,
    Med_Home_Value = B25077_001E
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Taking_Transit = (Transit_Commuters / Total_Commuters) * 100,
    Percent_White = (White_Pop / Total_Pop) * 100
  ) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)  #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83


```

## Map Philadelphia Context

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Map median income
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census4, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  # Stations 
  geom_point(
    data = indego4,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  mapTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
**Question** These data are in WGS84. If you wanted to calculate a spatial distance-based variable, like a buffer, should you use the dataset as it?
:::

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
No, you should not use the dataset in WGS84 to calculate spatial distance-based variables like buffers because WGS84 is a geographic coordinate system that measures locations in degrees rather than consistent distance units; this can lead to inaccurate distance calculations. Instead, you should first transform the data into a projected coordinate system (such as a local UTM or state plane projection) that uses linear units like meters or feet, which ensures that distances and buffers are calculated correctly.
:::

## Join Census Data to Stations

We'll spatially join census characteristics to each bike station.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Create sf object for stations
stations_sf4 <- indego4 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join to get census tract for each station
stations_census4 <- st_join(stations_sf4, philly_census4, left = TRUE) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

# Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts.

stations_for_map4 <- indego4 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census4 %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Add back to trip data
indego_census4 <- indego4 %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census4 %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )


# Prepare data for visualization
stations_for_map4 <- indego4 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census4 %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc))

# Create the map showing problem stations
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census4, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar,
    na.value = "grey90"
  ) +
  # Stations with census data (small grey dots)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map4 %>% filter(has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map4 %>% filter(!has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)",
    caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts"
  ) +
  mapTheme



```

## Dealing with missing data

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
We need to decide what to do with the non-residential bike share stations. For this example, we are going to remove them -- this is not necessarily the right way to do things always, but for the sake of simplicity, we are narrowing our scope to only stations in residential neighborhoods. We might opt to create a separate model for non-residential stations..
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Identify which stations to keep
valid_stations4 <- stations_census4 %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Filter trip data to valid stations only
indego_census4 <- indego4 %>%
  filter(start_station %in% valid_stations4) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census4 %>% 
      select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, 
             Percent_White, Total_Pop),
    by = "start_station"
  )

```

## Get Weather Data

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The date range for the weather data was updated using the riem_measures() function to retrieve hourly observations from the Philadelphia International Airport (PHL) station for the fourth quarter of 2025, specifically from October 1 to December 31. This adjustment ensures that the weather data is temporally aligned with the study period, allowing for more accurate analysis and integration with the trip and census datasets. By focusing only on this timeframe, the analysis captures relevant seasonal weather conditions, which helps improve the reliability of any conclusions regarding the relationship between weather patterns and travel behavior. Weather significantly affects bike share demand! Let's get hourly weather for Philadelphia.
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL)
# This covers Q4 2025: October 1 - December 31
weather_data4 <- riem_measures(
  station = "PHL",  # Philadelphia International Airport
  date_start = "2025-10-01",
  date_end = "2025-12-31"
)

# Process weather data
weather_processed4 <- weather_data4 %>%
  mutate(
    interval60 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"),
    Temperature = tmpf,  # Temperature in Fahrenheit
    Precipitation = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i),  # Hourly precip in inches
    Wind_Speed = sknt  # Wind speed in knots
  ) %>%
  select(interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed) %>%
  distinct(interval60, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed
weather_complete4 <- weather_processed4 %>%
  complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>%
  fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down")

# Look at the weather
summary(weather_complete4 %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed))

```


-   Check for any data structure changes? 

<div style="text-align: justify;"> Both Q1 and Q4 datasets show the exact same data structure: they contain the same four columns—interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, and Wind_Speed—in the same order, with identical timestamp formatting and hourly intervals. There are no added, removed, or rearranged fields, so structurally nothing has changed between the two datasets. The differences are purely in the data values themselves. The Q4 dataset has noticeably lower temperature values overall, includes some non-zero precipitation entries whereas the Q1 has only zeros, and shows a wider range and generally higher variation in wind speeds. These differences indicate that the datasets likely represent different conditions (such as a different time period), rather than any change in schema or data structure.</div>
 

## Visualize Weather Patterns


```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
ggplot(weather_complete4, aes(x = interval60, y = Temperature)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q4 2025",
    subtitle = "Winter to extreme winter transition",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The Q4 2025 temperature chart for Philadelphia shows a clear transition from fall into winter, which contrasts strongly with the warming pattern observed in Q1. In Q4, temperatures start relatively high in October, often ranging between 60°F and 75°F, but gradually decline over time, as indicated by the downward-sloping red trend line. By November, temperatures become cooler and more variable, generally falling between 45°F and 60°F, reflecting typical late autumn conditions. The most significant change occurs in December, where temperatures drop sharply, with many observations between 25°F and 40°F, and occasional dips even lower, signaling the onset of winter. Although there is a slight increase toward the end of December, the overall pattern remains dominated by colder conditions. In contrast to Q1—where temperatures begin low and steadily rise toward spring—Q4 demonstrates the opposite seasonal trend, moving from mild to increasingly cold conditions. This highlights how Q4 captures a cooling trajectory into winter, whereas Q1 represents a warming transition out of winter, with Q4 also showing slightly more pronounced variability during the early fall period.
:::

# CREATE SPACE-TIME PANEL

## Aggregate Trips to Station-Hour Level

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'

# Count trips by station-hour
trips_panel4 <- indego_census4 %>%
  group_by(interval60, start_station, start_lat, start_lon,
           Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop) %>%
  summarize(Trip_Count = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

# How many station-hour observations?
nrow(trips_panel4)

# How many unique stations?
length(unique(trips_panel4$start_station))

# How many unique hours?
length(unique(trips_panel4$interval60))

hist(trips_panel4$Trip_Count)
```

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
The histogram shows that the distribution of Trip_Count is heavily right-skewed, with most station-hour observations clustered at very low values, typically between 0 and 2 trips. As the number of trips increases, the frequency declines rapidly, with only a small number of observations exceeding 5 trips and very few reaching higher values up to around 25. This indicates that most stations experience low activity during most hours, while a small number of station-hour periods account for relatively high trip volumes.
:::

## Create Complete Panel Structure

::: {style="text-align: justify;"}
Not every station has trips every hour. We need a **complete panel** where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0).
:::

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Calculate expected panel size
n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel4$start_station))
n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel4$interval60))
expected_rows4 <- n_stations * n_hours

cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows4, big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel4), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows4 - nrow(trips_panel4), big.mark = ","), "\n")

# Create complete panel
study_panel4 <- expand_grid(
  interval60 = unique(trips_panel4$interval60),
  start_station = unique(trips_panel4$start_station)
) %>%
  # Join trip counts
  left_join(trips_panel4 %>% 
              select(interval60, start_station, Trip_Count),
            by = c("interval60", "start_station")) %>%
  # Replace NA trip counts with 0
  mutate(Trip_Count = replace_na(Trip_Count, 0))


hist(study_panel4$Trip_Count)

# Fill in station attributes (they're the same for all hours)
station_attributes4 <- trips_panel4 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  summarize(
    start_lat = first(start_lat),
    start_lon = first(start_lon),
    Med_Inc = first(Med_Inc),
    Percent_Taking_Transit = first(Percent_Taking_Transit),
    Percent_White = first(Percent_White),
    Total_Pop = first(Total_Pop)
  )




study_panel4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  left_join(station_attributes4, by = "start_station")



# Verify we have complete panel
cat("Complete panel rows:", format(nrow(study_panel4), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```

## Add Time Features

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
study_panel4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  mutate(
    week = week(interval60),
    month = month(interval60, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval60),
    date = as.Date(interval60),
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )



kable(head(study_panel4, 200), 
      caption = "Time Features",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
                full_width = TRUE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px", width = "100%")
```

## Join Weather Data

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
study_panel4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  left_join(weather_complete4, by = "interval60")



# Check for missing values
summary(study_panel4 %>% select(Trip_Count, Temperature, Precipitation))

 
kable(head(study_panel4, 10), 
      caption = "Joined Weather Data",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
                full_width = TRUE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px", width = "100%")

```

# CREATE TEMPORAL LAG VARIABLES

<div style="text-align: justify;"> The key innovation for space-time prediction: **past demand predicts future demand**.</div>

## Why Temporal Lags?

<div style="text-align: justify;">If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be \~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions.

**Note:** We still can't use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic.

Temporal lags: good Spatial lags: bad!</div>

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Sort by station and time
study_panel4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval60)

# Create lag variables WITHIN each station
study_panel4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1),
    lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2),
    lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3),
    lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12),
    lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station)
study_panel_complete4 <- study_panel4 %>%
  filter(!is.na(lag1day))


cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete4), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```
-Rows after removing NA lags: 596,232 
## Visualize Lag Correlations

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: 'hide'
# Sample one station to visualize
example_station4 <- study_panel_complete4 %>%
  filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>%
  head(168)  # One week

# Plot actual vs lagged demand
ggplot(example_station4, aes(x = interval60)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Current" = "#08519c",
    "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd",
    "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station",
    subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand",
    x = "Date-Time",
    y = "Trip Count",
    color = "Time Period"
  ) +
  plotTheme
```

# TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT

**CRITICAL:** We must train on PAST data and test on FUTURE data!

## Why Temporal Validation Matters

<div style="text-align: justify;"> Temporal validation matters in bike-share demand forecasting because the data has a natural time order that must be respected during model training and testing. If you were to randomly split the data into training and test sets — as is common in cross-sectional machine learning problems — you would risk training the model on future data and testing it on past data, which creates an artificially optimistic and misleading picture of model performance.
In this analysis, the split is made at week 49 as the threshold.
